text.title: Performance Testing Tools: k6 and Locust Core Rules
text.summary: 7 testable rules grounding the perf-test-tools methodology. Each rule pairs a binary check with sourced rationale; the final rule is the skip-this-methodology gate referenced by the decision tree.

rule.statement: Pick k6 for JS/TS teams (single binary, JS scripts). Pick Locust for Python teams (web UI, distributed runs).
rule.rationale: Tool match to language reduces context switching and on-call cost.

rule.statement: k6/Locust thresholds (p95, p99, error_rate) are the CI gate. Non-zero exit means PR fails.
rule.rationale: Without exit-on-threshold, perf jobs run as 'reports'; nobody reads reports.

rule.statement: Load tests use ramped stages (warmup, plateau, drain), not a flat VU count.
rule.rationale: Flat VU misses warmup behavior; ramped stages reveal cold-start regressions.

rule.statement: Both k6 and Locust scripts read thresholds from baselines.json — one source of truth.
rule.rationale: Threshold drift between tool config and baseline file causes false positives.

rule.statement: Perf runs hit an isolated staging environment with warm fixtures, not shared prod.
rule.rationale: Shared environments poison the measurement and risk DoS on prod.

rule.statement: Every perf run publishes an HTML report artifact to CI for human investigation.
rule.rationale: Exit code says pass/fail; the HTML report says why.

rule.statement: If baselines missing, browser timings needed, or staging unavailable — skip this methodology.
rule.rationale: k6/Locust pay back only when baselines + staging + CI hook exist.
